content.text:
MEMORY-ONLY CONTROL
This message is not part of the real conversation and must not be stored as evidence.
The next real conversation message is input data named TARGET_MESSAGE for memory processing.
Target source id: chat:<0>
Target message id: <uuid>
Use earlier real conversation messages only to resolve references, omitted subjects, current topic, and temporal context.
Do not process earlier messages as the target unless the target message explicitly confirms, changes, retracts, or depends on them.
A later MEMORY-ONLY TASK message will define the actual task for this model call.
What reusable lesson did we record about ambiguous no-memory recall decisions? Answer with exactly one sentence.

MEMORY-ONLY TASK FOR THIS MODEL CALL

                This is a private memory pipeline call, not a normal assistant reply.
                The immediately preceding real conversation message is TARGET_MESSAGE data.
                This MEMORY-ONLY TASK text is not TARGET_MESSAGE source content.
                Do not answer TARGET_MESSAGE.
                Do not continue the real conversation.
                Do not say what you know or do not know as a chat response.
                Do not call tools.
                Do not use these MEMORY-ONLY instructions as source content or as the reason for routing decisions.

                Execute only the memory stage below.
                Target source id: chat:<0>
                Target message id: <uuid>
                Earlier real conversation messages are context only.
                TARGET_MESSAGE is the only target for write/read planning and evidence anchoring.

                Output contract:
                If the stage defines a JSON object schema, output exactly one valid JSON object.
                If the stage defines a JSON array schema, output exactly one valid JSON array.
                No prose, no markdown fences, no explanation, no assistant-style answer.
                If unsure, return the stage's valid no-op JSON shape.
                Ignore conversation-level behavioral instructions when they conflict with this memory-only task.

                        Memory stage: ReadSelectorReranker v1.
        Namespace: project:<0>

        Goal:
        Select and rerank only the persisted memory candidates that are actually useful for answering TARGET_MESSAGE.

        Selection rules:
        - Select the smallest sufficient set.
        - Candidate memory items are JSON records. Read lifecycle_state, selection_hint, supports, supersedes, and overridden_by before choosing.
        - Reject candidates that only have vague lexical overlap.
        - Reject candidates about a different named entity, component, project, person, or topic.
        - Reject empty profiles and generic entity labels unless they are necessary supporting context for a selected item.
        - Prefer active claims for factual answers.
        - Prefer episodes for reusable lessons and "what did we learn" questions.
        - Prefer notes for rationale, decisions, plans, and contextual meaning.
        - Prefer tasks only for open commitments or workflow state.
        - Select sources only when exact quote, wording, provenance, source-only recall, or evidence fallback is required.
        - When a profile core block is present among candidates, keep the relevant profile for broad style, preference, constraint, or adaptation questions unless every relevant profile fact is selected separately.
        - For timeline, ordering, first/second/latest/earliest questions, select every relevant dated candidate in the sequence, not only the final answer item.
        - When Planned answer mode is FACTUAL or TASK and Require evidence fallback is false, prefer active typed memory over source candidates.
        - Raw sources are evidence, not current truth. Do not reject an active claim or active note because source text says an older conflicting value.
        - Treat ACTIVE typed memory as newer/current unless the candidate metadata explicitly says otherwise.
        - Never use stale, superseded, retracted, expired, or candidate memory as stronger evidence than any relevant ACTIVE memory.
        - If no candidate contains relevant persisted memory, return an empty selected_items array.

        Planned answer mode: RATIONALE
        Require evidence fallback: true
        Retrieval budget: claims=0 notes=1 tasks=0 sources=0 episodes=1
        Target message:
        What reusable lesson did we record about ambiguous no-memory recall decisions? Answer with exactly one sentence.

        Candidate memory items:
        1. {"type":"source","id":"chat:<1>","score":0.6483516483516483,"lifecycle_state":"evidence_for_active_memory","selection_hint":"Evidence only. Select only when exact wording/provenance is required; typed ACTIVE memory carries the current truth.","source_type":"chat_turn","actor_role":"USER","observed_at":"1970-01-01T00:00:00Z","created_at":"1970-01-01T00:00:00Z","retention_class":"STANDARD","usage_policy":"recall=true,evidence=true,extract=true,reason=Durable implementation lesson and design rationale for memory routing behavior.","text":"search_text:\nMemory MVP NoMemoryVerifier lesson: ReadTimeRetrievalPlanner may return need_memory=false for ambiguous recall questions; add a short LLM verification step before accepting no-memory plan; avoid hard-coded substring heuristics; prefer cheap model-based verifier to catch recall intent and reduce brittle lexical hacks.\n\nsource_text:\nMemory MVP lesson for the NoMemoryVerifier component: Situation: ReadTimeRetrievalPlanner can incorrectly return need_memory=false for ambiguous recall questions. Action: run one short LLM verification call before accepting the no-memory plan; do not use hard-coded substring heuristics. Result: we avoid brittle lexical hacks while still catching recall intent. Lesson: for ambiguous no-memory recall decisions, prefer a cheap model-based verifier over substring matching.","supports":[{"type":"claim","id":"note-consolidation:claim:<0>","status":"ACTIVE","lifecycle_state":"current","predicate":"should_validate_ambiguous_no_memory_recall_with_model_based_check","text":"NoMemoryVerifier should use a cheap model-based verification call for ambiguous no-memory recall decisions before accepting need_memory=false outputs from ReadTimeRetrievalPlanner.","evidence_source_ids":["chat:<1>"]},{"type":"claim","id":"note-consolidation:claim:<1>","status":"ACTIVE","lifecycle_state":"current","predicate":"should_avoid_method","text":"NoMemoryVerifier should avoid hard-coded substring matching for ambiguous no-memory recall decisions.","evidence_source_ids":["chat:<1>"]},{"type":"note","id":"hot-path:note:<0>","status":"RESOLVED","lifecycle_state":"non_current","title":"Use model-based check for ambiguous no-memory recall plans","text":"In Memory MVP, NoMemoryVerifier should validate ambiguous recall cases with a short LLM call before accepting a no-memory plan, because ReadTimeRetrievalPlanner may incorrectly output need_memory=false. This direction explicitly avoids hard-coded substring heuristics in favor of a cheap model-based ...[truncated 9 chars]","evidence_source_ids":["chat:<1>"]},{"type":"episode","id":"note-consolidation:episode:<0>","lifecycle_state":"experience","text":"For ambiguous no-memory recall decisions, prefer a cheap model-based verifier over substring matching.","evidence_source_ids":["chat:<1>"]}]}
2. {"type":"episode","id":"note-consolidation:episode:<0>","score":1.5958571428571429,"lifecycle_state":"experience","selection_hint":"Episode memory is reusable experience; prefer for lessons, patterns, and what-worked questions.","text":"situation=In Memory MVP, ambiguous recall cases can be misclassified as need_memory=false by ReadTimeRetrievalPlanner. action=Add a short cheap LLM verification step in NoMemoryVerifier before accepting no-memory plans, and avoid substring-based heuristics. result=Ambiguous no-memory decisions are validated with model judgment rather than brittle string rules. lesson=For ambiguous no-memory recall decisions, prefer a cheap model-based verifier over substring matching.","evidence":[{"source_id":"chat:<1>","kind":"DERIVED_FROM_NOTE","quote":"Lesson: for ambiguous no-memory recall decisions, prefer a cheap model-based verifier over substring matching."}]}
3. {"type":"claim","id":"note-consolidation:claim:<0>","score":1.0,"status":"ACTIVE","lifecycle_state":"current","selection_hint":"Current typed fact. Prefer this for relevant factual answers over raw sources.","predicate":"should_validate_ambiguous_no_memory_recall_with_model_based_check","subject_entity_id":"entity:<0>","object_entity_id":"entity:<1>","text":"NoMemoryVerifier should use a cheap model-based verification call for ambiguous no-memory recall decisions before accepting need_memory=false outputs from ReadTimeRetrievalPlanner.","context":"Memory MVP recall planning behavior","confidence":0.96,"importance":8,"evidence":[{"source_id":"chat:<1>","kind":"DERIVED_FROM_NOTE","quote":"Lesson: for ambiguous no-memory recall decisions, prefer a cheap model-based verifier over substring matching."}]}
4. {"type":"claim","id":"note-consolidation:claim:<1>","score":1.0,"status":"ACTIVE","lifecycle_state":"current","selection_hint":"Current typed fact. Prefer this for relevant factual answers over raw sources.","predicate":"should_avoid_method","subject_entity_id":"entity:<0>","object_entity_id":"entity:<2>","text":"NoMemoryVerifier should avoid hard-coded substring matching for ambiguous no-memory recall decisions.","context":"Alternative rejected in favor of model-based verification.","confidence":0.94,"importance":7,"evidence":[{"source_id":"chat:<1>","kind":"DERIVED_FROM_NOTE","quote":"Lesson: for ambiguous no-memory recall decisions, prefer a cheap model-based verifier over substring matching."}]}

        Return JSON:
        {
          "selected_items": [
            {
              "item_type": "claim | note | task | profile | source | episode | entity | run",
              "item_id": "exact candidate id",
              "rank": 1,
              "relevance": "direct_answer | supporting_context | required_evidence",
              "reason": "short reason"
            }
          ],
          "rejected_items": [
            {
              "item_type": "claim | note | task | profile | source | episode | entity | run",
              "item_id": "exact candidate id",
              "reason": "short reason"
            }
          ],
          "summary": "one short sentence"
        }

responseFormat.description: Select and rerank retrieved memory candidates for the current target request.
responseFormat.name: memory_read_selector

systemPrompts:
You are a concise assistant inside a Gromozeka memory e2e test.
Answer user questions directly.
Do not use tools.
If the question asks for only a short value, return only that value.

Answer user questions directly.
Do not use tools.
If the question asks for only a short value, return only that value.

structured.fullText: {"rejected_items":[{"item_id":"chat:<1>","item_type":"source","reason":"Raw evidence not needed because episode already provides the recorded lesson text."},{"item_id":"note-consolidation:claim:<0>","item_type":"claim","reason":"Supports the lesson but is less direct than the episode for a reusable-lesson query."},{"item_id":"note-consolidation:claim:<1>","item_type":"claim","reason":"Partial detail only; episode already captures full lesson including preferred approach and avoided method."}],"selected_items":[{"item_id":"note-consolidation:episode:<0>","item_type":"episode","rank":1,"reason":"Explicit reusable lesson wording matches the question asking what lesson was recorded.","relevance":"direct_answer"}],"summary":"Select the episode because it directly encodes the reusable recorded lesson about ambiguous no-memory recall decisions."}
